stem: ARDS Berlin criteria require all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: This IS required for severity grading.
This IS required — bilateral opacities not explained by other causes.
This IS required.

Correct! ARDS requires PCWP ≤18 (non-cardiogenic). Elevated PCWP (>18) indicates cardiogenic pulmonary edema, not ARDS. Need to exclude heart failure with echo/BNP. Other criteria: within 1 week, bilateral opacities, P/F ratio classification on PEEP ≥5.

explanation: The Berlin criteria for ARDS require: (1) Acute onset within 1 week of a known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms; (2) Bilateral opacities on chest imaging (CXR or CT) not fully explained by effusions, atelectasis, or nodules; (3) Respiratory failure NOT fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (may require echocardiography to exclude); (4) Hypoxemia classified by PaO2/FiO2 ratio on PEEP ≥5 cmH2O — Mild: 200-300, Moderate: 100-200, Severe: ≤100. An elevated PCWP >18 mmHg would suggest cardiogenic pulmonary edema, NOT ARDS. The Berlin criteria replaced the older AECC definition and specifically eliminated the requirement for PCWP measurement, instead requiring that respiratory failure not be 'fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload.' ARDS and heart failure can coexist, but PCWP >18 as a standalone criterion is NOT part of the Berlin definition — it is the OPPOSITE of what Berlin requires.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: ARDS is defined by acute onset, bilateral lung infiltrates on chest radiography or CT scan of a non-cardiac origin, and PaO2/FiO2 ratio

verifiedExplanation: The Berlin criteria for ARDS require: (1) Acute onset within 1 week of a known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms; (2) Bilateral opacities on chest imaging (CXR or CT) not fully explained by effusions, atelectasis, or nodules; (3) Respiratory failure NOT fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (may require echocardiography to exclude); (4) Hypoxemia classified by PaO2/FiO2 ratio on PEEP ≥5 cmH2O — Mild: 200-300, Moderate: 100-200, Severe: ≤100. An elevated PCWP >18 mmHg would suggest cardiogenic pulmonary edema, NOT ARDS. The Berlin criteria replaced the older AECC definition and specifically eliminated the requirement for PCWP measurement, instead requiring that respiratory failure not be 'fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload.' ARDS and heart failure can coexist, but PCWP >18 as a standalone criterion is NOT part of the Berlin definition — it is the OPPOSITE of what Berlin requires.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

stem: ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation uses tidal volume of:

choices.feedback: Too low — causes inadequate ventilation and severe hypercapnia.

Correct! Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg IBW) is the cornerstone of ARDS management. ARDSNet trial: reduced mortality from 40% to 31%. Target plateau pressure ≤30 cmH₂O. Accept permissive hypercapnia (pH >7.25). Calculate IBW from height, not actual weight.
This is the traditional (harmful) tidal volume that causes volutrauma.
Far too high — causes barotrauma and worsens ARDS.

explanation: The ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation protocol uses a target tidal volume of 6 mL/kg ideal body weight (IBW). StatPearls states the protocol sets 'tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight' with 6 mL/kg as the target. This strategy reduces ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) by minimizing volutrauma (overdistension of alveoli) and barotrauma (excessive airway pressures). The landmark ARMA trial (NEJM 2000) demonstrated a 22% relative reduction in mortality with 6 mL/kg IBW vs 12 mL/kg IBW (31% vs 39.8%). Other key ARDSNet parameters: plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, respiratory rate up to 35, SpO2 88-95%, pH goal 7.30-7.45. CRITICAL: tidal volume is calculated using IDEAL body weight (based on height and sex), NOT actual body weight — obese patients receive the same tidal volume as non-obese patients of the same height.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW), respiratory rate up to 35 bpm, SpO2 88% to 95%, plateau pressure less than 30 cm H2O

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Prone positioning in ARDS is indicated when P/F ratio is:

choices.feedback: Correct! PROSEVA trial: prone positioning for ≥16h/day in severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality from 33% to 16% (~50% reduction). Most impactful non-ventilator intervention. Mechanism: improves V/Q matching, reduces compression atelectasis, improves secretion drainage.
Mild ARDS does not require proning.
Proning is reserved for moderate-severe ARDS, not mild.
P/F >300 doesn't meet ARDS criteria.

explanation: Prone positioning in ARDS is indicated when the P/F ratio is <150 (moderate-to-severe ARDS) despite optimized ventilator settings. The PROSEVA trial (Guérin et al., NEJM 2013) demonstrated that prone positioning for ≥16 hours/day in patients with severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality from 32.8% to 16% (NNT = 6). StatPearls notes prone positioning 'has shown benefits in about 50% to 70% of patients' and improves oxygenation by recruiting dependent lung zones, improving diaphragmatic excursion, and increasing functional residual capacity. Prone positioning is NOT indicated for mild ARDS (P/F 200-300) or patients without ARDS (P/F >300). 'Any P/F ratio' is too broad — the evidence supports benefit specifically in moderate-severe disease.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Prone position has shown benefits in about 50% to 70% of patients. The improvement in oxygenation is rapid and allows a reduction in FiO2 and PEEP

verifiedExplanation: Prone positioning in ARDS is indicated when the P/F ratio is <150 (moderate-to-severe ARDS) despite optimized ventilator settings. The PROSEVA trial (Guérin et al., NEJM 2013) demonstrated that prone positioning for ≥16 hours/day in patients with severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality from 32.8% to 16% (NNT = 6). StatPearls notes prone positioning 'has shown benefits in about 50% to 70% of patients' and improves oxygenation by recruiting dependent lung zones, improving diaphragmatic excursion, and increasing functional residual capacity. Prone positioning is NOT indicated for mild ARDS (P/F 200-300) or patients without ARDS (P/F >300). 'Any P/F ratio' is too broad — the evidence supports benefit specifically in moderate-severe disease.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

choices.feedback: Trauma is an indirect cause but not #1.
PE can contribute but is not a primary cause of ARDS.

Correct! Pneumonia is the #1 direct (pulmonary) cause. Sepsis is the #1 indirect (extrapulmonary) cause. Together they account for the majority of ARDS cases. Other causes: aspiration, pancreatitis, massive transfusion (TRALI), burns.
Heart failure causes cardiogenic pulmonary edema, which must be excluded to diagnose ARDS.

explanation: Pneumonia is the #1 cause of ARDS, accounting for approximately 60% of cases (with or without sepsis). AAFP data confirms 'Most cases of ARDS in adults are associated with pneumonia with or without sepsis (60%) or with nonpulmonary sepsis (16%).' Other causes include aspiration, inhalation injury, pancreatitis, trauma, transfusion (TRALI), drug toxicity, and burns. Trauma is a recognized cause but less common than pneumonia. Pulmonary embolism can cause respiratory failure but is NOT a classic ARDS trigger (it causes V/Q mismatch, not diffuse alveolar damage). Heart failure causes cardiogenic pulmonary edema, which is explicitly EXCLUDED from the ARDS definition — ARDS is by definition non-cardiogenic.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: ARDS is an acute disorder...in the absence of any evidence of cardiogenic pulmonary edema

verifiedExplanation: Pneumonia is the #1 cause of ARDS, accounting for approximately 60% of cases (with or without sepsis). AAFP data confirms 'Most cases of ARDS in adults are associated with pneumonia with or without sepsis (60%) or with nonpulmonary sepsis (16%).' Other causes include aspiration, inhalation injury, pancreatitis, trauma, transfusion (TRALI), drug toxicity, and burns. Trauma is a recognized cause but less common than pneumonia. Pulmonary embolism can cause respiratory failure but is NOT a classic ARDS trigger (it causes V/Q mismatch, not diffuse alveolar damage). Heart failure causes cardiogenic pulmonary edema, which is explicitly EXCLUDED from the ARDS definition — ARDS is by definition non-cardiogenic.

stem: Conservative vs liberal fluid strategy in ARDS (FACTT trial):

choices.feedback: Liberal fluids worsen pulmonary edema in ARDS.

Correct! FACTT trial: conservative fluid strategy improved oxygenation and shortened ventilator days without increasing organ failure. ARDS lungs are 'leaky' — extra fluid worsens edema. Target CVP <4 or PCWP <8. Diuretics (furosemide) when hemodynamically stable.
Conservative strategy showed significant benefit.
The trial compared fluid volume strategies, not fluid types.

explanation: The FACTT trial (Fluid and Catheter Treatment Trial, NEJM 2006) demonstrated that a conservative (restrictive) fluid strategy improves ventilator-free days and ICU-free days compared to a liberal fluid strategy in ARDS, without increasing non-pulmonary organ failures. Conservative fluid management aims to keep the patient 'dry' — achieving even to negative fluid balance using diuretics and fluid restriction. The rationale: ARDS involves increased pulmonary capillary permeability, so excess intravascular volume worsens pulmonary edema and impairs gas exchange. Liberal fluids would worsen pulmonary edema. There was no mortality difference at 60 days, but the significant improvement in ventilator-free days (14.6 vs 11.2 days) makes conservative strategy the standard of care. Colloids vs crystalloids was not the focus of this trial.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: careful management of fluids in high-risk patients can be helpful

verifiedExplanation: The FACTT trial (Fluid and Catheter Treatment Trial, NEJM 2006) demonstrated that a conservative (restrictive) fluid strategy improves ventilator-free days and ICU-free days compared to a liberal fluid strategy in ARDS, without increasing non-pulmonary organ failures. Conservative fluid management aims to keep the patient 'dry' — achieving even to negative fluid balance using diuretics and fluid restriction. The rationale: ARDS involves increased pulmonary capillary permeability, so excess intravascular volume worsens pulmonary edema and impairs gas exchange. Liberal fluids would worsen pulmonary edema. There was no mortality difference at 60 days, but the significant improvement in ventilator-free days (14.6 vs 11.2 days) makes conservative strategy the standard of care. Colloids vs crystalloids was not the focus of this trial.

stem: P/F ratio of 80 on PEEP 10 classifies ARDS as:

choices.feedback: Correct! P/F ≤100 on PEEP ≥5 = severe ARDS (mortality ~45%). P/F ratio = PaO2 ÷ FiO2. Example: PaO2 80 on FiO2 1.0 = P/F 80 = severe. This patient needs: low tidal volume, prone positioning, possible paralysis, consider ECMO if refractory.
Moderate = P/F 100-200.
P/F 80 with bilateral opacities and appropriate timing meets severe ARDS criteria.

explanation: A P/F ratio of 80 on PEEP ≥5 classifies as SEVERE ARDS per the Berlin criteria. The Berlin classification: Mild ARDS: P/F 200-300 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~27%); Moderate ARDS: P/F 100-200 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~35%); Severe ARDS: P/F ≤100 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~45%). A P/F of 80 is well below the ≤100 threshold for severe ARDS. This patient requires lung-protective ventilation (target rate IBW), high PEEP, prone positioning (≥16 hours/day), neuromuscular blockade consideration, and potentially ECMO if refractory. The PEEP requirement of ≥5 cmH2O is specified because some degree of positive pressure is needed to standardize the measurement — this patient on PEEP 10 clearly meets this criterion.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Berlin definition: PaO2/FiO2 categories of ≤100 (severe), 101-200 (moderate), and 201-300 (mild) on PEEP ≥5

verifiedExplanation: A P/F ratio of 80 on PEEP ≥5 classifies as SEVERE ARDS per the Berlin criteria. The Berlin classification: Mild ARDS: P/F 200-300 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~27%); Moderate ARDS: P/F 100-200 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~35%); Severe ARDS: P/F ≤100 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~45%). A P/F of 80 is well below the ≤100 threshold for severe ARDS. This patient requires lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW), high PEEP, prone positioning (≥16 hours/day), neuromuscular blockade consideration, and potentially ECMO if refractory. The PEEP requirement of ≥5 cmH2O is specified because some degree of positive pressure is needed to standardize the measurement — this patient on PEEP 10 clearly meets this criterion.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

stem: ECMO in ARDS is reserved for:

choices.feedback: ECMO is rescue, not first-line. Start with ARDSNet ventilation + proning.

choices.text: Refractory hypoxemia despite optimal conventional management
choices.feedback: Correct! ECMO (veno-venous for respiratory failure) is a rescue therapy when optimal ventilation, proning, paralysis, and inhaled vasodilators fail. EOLIA trial showed trend toward benefit. Consider when P/F <80 despite ≥6h optimal management. Best outcomes at experienced ECMO centers.
ECMO is never prophylactic.
ECMO is resource-intensive and reserved for refractory cases.

explanation: ECMO (Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation) in ARDS is reserved for refractory hypoxemia despite optimal conventional management — it is a rescue therapy, not first-line. ECMO is considered when patients remain severely hypoxemic (P/F <80 or <60 despite optimal ventilator settings, prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade, and high PEEP). The EOLIA trial (Combes et al., NEJM 2018) suggested a trend toward benefit in severe ARDS refractory to conventional management. VV-ECMO (veno-venous) provides gas exchange support while allowing ultra-lung-protective ventilation, reducing VILI. ECMO requires specialized centers and carries risks including hemorrhage, thrombosis, infection, and hemolysis. It is NOT first-line for severe ARDS (lung-protective ventilation + prone positioning are first), NOT for mild ARDS, and NOT for all moderate ARDS patients.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]
verifiedExplanation: ECMO (Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation) in ARDS is reserved for refractory hypoxemia despite optimal conventional management — it is a rescue therapy, not first-line. ECMO is considered when patients remain severely hypoxemic (P/F <80 or <60 despite optimal ventilator settings, prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade, and high PEEP). The EOLIA trial (Combes et al., NEJM 2018) suggested a trend toward benefit in severe ARDS refractory to conventional management. VV-ECMO (veno-venous) provides gas exchange support while allowing ultra-lung-protective ventilation, reducing VILI. ECMO requires specialized centers and carries risks including hemorrhage, thrombosis, infection, and hemolysis. It is NOT first-line for severe ARDS (lung-protective ventilation + prone positioning are first), NOT for mild ARDS, and NOT for all moderate ARDS patients.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

stem: Permissive hypercapnia in ARDS means accepting:

choices.feedback: Low tidal volumes may lead to high (not low) pCO2.

choices.text: Higher pCO2 levels to keep tidal volumes low (pH >7.25)
choices.feedback: Correct! Low tidal volumes may not fully clear CO2 → pCO2 rises. This is acceptable as long as pH stays >7.25. The mortality benefit of low tidal volumes far outweighs the risk of mild hypercapnia. Exception: contraindicated in elevated ICP (hypercapnia → cerebral vasodilation → raised ICP).
Impossible and not a goal.

choices.text: Normal pCO2 with high tidal volumes
choices.feedback: High tidal volumes cause volutrauma — the entire point is to accept higher pCO2 for lung protection.

explanation: Permissive hypercapnia means accepting higher-than-normal pCO2 levels in order to keep tidal volumes low and prevent ventilator-induced lung injury, provided the pH remains above 7.25 (some protocols accept ≥7.20). When using low tidal volumes (target rate IBW), CO2 clearance may be insufficient, leading to respiratory acidosis. Rather than increasing tidal volume (which would cause volutrauma in stiff ARDS lungs), the strategy 'permits' elevated pCO2 levels — prioritizing lung protection over normal blood gas values. The ARDSNet protocol targets pH 7.30-7.45 but allows respiratory rate increases up to 35 bpm to compensate before accepting hypercapnia. Low pCO2 with high tidal volumes would defeat the purpose of lung protection. 'Zero pCO2' is physiologically impossible. 'Normal pCO2 with high tidal volumes' describes the pre-ARDSNet approach that caused more lung injury and higher mortality.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight, pH goal 7.30 to 7.45

verifiedExplanation: Permissive hypercapnia means accepting higher-than-normal pCO2 levels in order to keep tidal volumes low and prevent ventilator-induced lung injury, provided the pH remains above 7.25 (some protocols accept ≥7.20). When using low tidal volumes (6 mL/kg IBW), CO2 clearance may be insufficient, leading to respiratory acidosis. Rather than increasing tidal volume (which would cause volutrauma in stiff ARDS lungs), the strategy 'permits' elevated pCO2 levels — prioritizing lung protection over normal blood gas values. The ARDSNet protocol targets pH 7.30-7.45 but allows respiratory rate increases up to 35 bpm to compensate before accepting hypercapnia. Low pCO2 with high tidal volumes would defeat the purpose of lung protection. 'Zero pCO2' is physiologically impossible. 'Normal pCO2 with high tidal volumes' describes the pre-ARDSNet approach that caused more lung injury and higher mortality.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

stem: ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation recommends which tidal volume strategy?

choices.text: Low tidal volume based on ideal body weight
choices.feedback: Correct! Low tidal volume ventilation is the cornerstone of ARDS management. The ARDSNet trial demonstrated reduced mortality compared to conventional higher volumes. Tidal volume is calculated from ideal body weight, not actual weight.

choices.text: High tidal volume to maximize oxygenation
choices.feedback: High tidal volumes cause ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) through overdistension of remaining functional alveoli (baby lung concept). This increases mortality.

choices.text: Tidal volume based on actual body weight
choices.feedback: Using actual body weight overestimates lung size in obese patients, leading to overdistension. Ideal body weight (based on height and sex) correlates with lung size.

choices.text: Variable tidal volumes titrated to plateau pressure only
choices.feedback: While plateau pressure <30 cmH2O is a target, tidal volume based on IBW is the primary ARDSNet recommendation. Both parameters are monitored together.

explanation: The ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation protocol uses low tidal volumes based on ideal body weight (IBW) as its cornerstone. The landmark ARMA trial showed a 22% relative reduction in mortality. IBW is used because lung size correlates with height and sex, not actual weight. Plateau pressure is kept below 30 cmH2O to minimize barotrauma. Permissive hypercapnia may result from low tidal volumes but is generally tolerated if pH remains above 7.25. PEEP tables guide FiO2/PEEP combinations to maintain oxygenation.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW), respiratory rate up to 35 bpm, SpO2 88% to 95%, plateau pressure less than 30 cm H2O

verifiedExplanation: The ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation protocol uses a target tidal volume of 6 mL/kg ideal body weight (IBW). StatPearls states the protocol sets 'tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight' with 6 mL/kg as the target. This strategy reduces ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) by minimizing volutrauma (overdistension of alveoli) and barotrauma (excessive airway pressures). The landmark ARMA trial (NEJM 2000) demonstrated a 22% relative reduction in mortality with 6 mL/kg IBW vs 12 mL/kg IBW (31% vs 39.8%). Other key ARDSNet parameters: plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, respiratory rate up to 35, SpO2 88-95%, pH goal 7.30-7.45. CRITICAL: tidal volume is calculated using IDEAL body weight (based on height and sex), NOT actual body weight — obese patients receive the same tidal volume as non-obese patients of the same height.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]
